etymology.description: phonetic 辡 (two prisoners accusing each other) + semantic 瓜 (melon) -> differentiation; originally referred to melon seeds -> petal
etymology.interpretation: The reduplication of the knife in 辡 highlights the image of separating parts or distinguishing sections, with 瓜 (melon) suggesting something that is segmented, much like melon seeds. The character originally described the splitting of melons and by extension their seeds, eventually coming to mean any natural segment like a petal.

examples.chinese: 每朵花有五片花瓣(每朵花有五片花瓣)。
examples.pinyin: Měi duǒ huā yǒu wǔ piàn huābàn.

examples.chinese: 她轻轻地擦去一瓣花瓣上的露水(她輕輕地擦去一瓣花瓣上的露水)。
examples.pinyin: Tā qīngqīng de cā qù yī bàn huābàn shàng de lùshuǐ.
examples.english: She gently wiped the dew off a petal.

examples.chinese: 橘子的每一瓣果肉都很甜(橘子的每一瓣果肉都很甜)。
examples.pinyin: Júzi de měi yī bàn guǒròu dōu hěn tián.
examples.english: Each segment of the orange is very sweet.
